skill.name: tasknotes
skill.description: Manage Obsidian TaskNotes tasks through a local HTTP API and command line helper.
skill.summary: Create, list, update, and delete Obsidian TaskNotes tasks from an AI assistant workflow.
skill.author: ArtemXTech

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found network access, environment variable access, documented shell commands, and several weak-crypto heuristics. Review confirms the skill uses a local TaskNotes HTTP API and optional bearer token, while the weak-crypto and prompt-injection signals are false positives. Publication is acceptable with a warning because API credentials are read from .env and sent over plain HTTP to the configured local endpoint.

medium_findings.title: Optional API Token Sent Over Plain HTTP
medium_findings.description: The script loads TASKNOTES_API_KEY from a vault .env file and adds it as a bearer token for requests to the TaskNotes API. The target is intended to be localhost, but plain HTTP and an unvalidated port configuration make credential exposure possible if local configuration is changed or traffic is intercepted on the host.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly reads an API token and sends it in an Authorization header on HTTP requests. The intended localhost use lowers confidence of malicious intent, but the credential-handling risk is real.

medium_findings.title: Task Data Sent To Local HTTP API
medium_findings.description: Task titles, details, project names, dates, contexts, and task identifiers are transmitted to the TaskNotes HTTP API. This is core functionality, but it can expose private task content to any process serving the configured local endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The request payload is built directly from user task fields and sent through api_request. This matches the skill purpose, but it carries sensitive personal task data.

low_findings.title: Documented Shell Commands Are Expected Usage
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains uv run examples for invoking the bundled CLI. These are documentation examples rather than dynamic shell execution in code, so command execution findings are mostly false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are fenced command examples in documentation. They do execute the local script when followed, but no dynamic command construction is present there.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Heuristics Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography matches refer to markdown syntax, date examples, or argparse help text rather than cryptographic functions. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms being used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found task descriptions, markdown API documentation, and command-line argument definitions. There is no cryptographic operation at those locations.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Heuristics Are URL Encoding And Task Queries
low_findings.description: Static reconnaissance matches point to URL encoding of task identifiers and normal TaskNotes list or options commands. No evidence found of host, network, or system discovery behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code quotes a task path for a URL and calls documented TaskNotes endpoints. The behavior is scoped to task management, not reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment-Derived HTTP Endpoint And Authorization Header
dangerous_patterns.description: BASE_URL is built from TASKNOTES_API_PORT and every request may include an Authorization bearer token. The code should validate that the port is numeric and that requests remain bound to localhost.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and header construction are explicit in code. The risk depends on local configuration, so confidence is high for the pattern and lower for exploitability.

content.value_statement: Task work in Obsidian can be hard to manage from an AI chat. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a local CLI for TaskNotes task operations.
actual_capabilities: List TaskNotes tasks with filters for status, project, priority, overdue state, and result limit.
Create tasks with title, project, priority, status, due date, scheduled time, contexts, estimate, and details.
Update existing tasks by file path with status, priority, title, dates, or details.
Delete tasks through the TaskNotes HTTP API when given a task file path.
Show available TaskNotes projects, statuses, and priorities.
Return either JSON output for agents or table output for people.
limitations: Requires Obsidian with the TaskNotes plugin and HTTP API enabled.
Only works with the local TaskNotes API endpoint configured by the user.
Does not validate that configured status, priority, or project values exist before sending requests.
Can expose task content and API tokens to the local HTTP endpoint.

use_cases.title: Daily Task Review
use_cases.description: List active or overdue tasks from an Obsidian vault before planning the day.

use_cases.title: Project Task Capture
use_cases.description: Create linked TaskNotes items while discussing a project plan with an AI assistant.

use_cases.title: Agent Handoff Tracking
use_cases.description: Update task status and details after an AI coding or research session completes.

prompt_templates.title: List Today Tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my current TaskNotes tasks in a table and highlight anything overdue.

prompt_templates.title: Create Project Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TaskNotes task called "Prepare launch checklist" for project "Website Redesign" with high priority.

prompt_templates.title: Update Task Status
prompt_templates.prompt: Mark the TaskNotes item at "Tasks/prepare-launch-checklist.md" as done and add a short completion note.

prompt_templates.title: Plan From Filters
prompt_templates.prompt: List in-progress and high priority TaskNotes items, then suggest the next three tasks to schedule.

output_examples.output: A concise table of matching tasks with status, priority, title, project, and total count.

output_examples.input: Create a high priority task for the Website Redesign project.
output_examples.output: Confirmation that the task was created, including its title, path, status, and priority.

output_examples.input: What status and priority values can I use?
output_examples.output: A readable list of configured projects, statuses, and priorities from TaskNotes.

best_practices: Enable a TaskNotes API token and keep it in a vault .env file with limited access.
Run the options command before using custom status, priority, or project values.
Review task details before sending private personal or work information through the local API.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill when Obsidian or the TaskNotes HTTP API is not running.
Do not point the API configuration at remote or untrusted endpoints.
Do not store sensitive credentials or secrets inside task titles or task details.

faq.question: Does this skill require Obsidian?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires Obsidian with the TaskNotes plugin installed and its HTTP API enabled.

faq.question: Can it create new tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. It can create tasks with project, priority, status, dates, contexts, estimates, and details.

faq.question: Can it update existing tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. It updates tasks by TaskNotes file path, such as a path under the Tasks folder.

faq.question: Does it connect to the internet?
faq.answer: It is designed for a local HTTP API on localhost, not a public internet service.

faq.question: Does it need an API key?
faq.answer: Only if the TaskNotes HTTP API is configured to require authentication.

faq.question: Is task content private?
faq.answer: Task content is sent to the configured local HTTP endpoint, so users should keep the endpoint local and trusted.
